description: Game design MCQ from university course — with human CBM data (zero contamination risk)

questions.question: What do we mean by meaningful play?
choices: That the rules of the game provide a fair contest
That there is an enjoyable outcome from the game
That the game has strong narrative content
That it is easy to link your actions to consequences
That the play results in meaning
questions.subject: game_design

questions.question: Which of the following shows the difference between emergent and progression based rules?
choices: Emergent rules are generated by discussions within the design team, while progression rules are determined by publishers
Progress rules are about scoring points, emergent rules are about developing the story
Progress rules are linked to events, emergent rules rely on the interaction in a simple set of rules
Progress rules help the player get further in the game, while emergent rules punish them to provide game balance
Emergent rules are created at the beginning of the design process while progress rules are a result of feedback from testing
questions.subject: game_design

questions.question: A good UI?
choices: Is more important in casual games than hardcore games
Is familiar without being boring
Provides all the necessary functions but no more
Improves the game mechanics
Stops being noticed
questions.subject: game_design

questions.question: Ludology is?
choices: The study of balancing
The study of game design
The study of games
The study of lewd behavior in visual media
The study of programming
questions.subject: game_design

questions.question: Which one of these is not a common feature of games in the RTS category:
choices: Micro management
Strategy
Realtime interaction
Tech tree development
Character development
questions.subject: game_design

questions.question: The technical definition of feedback is
choices: When the output of a system is used as part of the input in the next time interval
What the player sees
The system associated with awarding a score and accomplishments
The ratings provided by critics and online users rating systems
The responses from play-testers during the alpha and beta testing phases of game development
questions.subject: game_design

questions.question: Which of these is not an example of negative feedback?
choices: The blue shell in Mario kart
Rubber banding in racing car games
Increasing experience required to achieve higher levels
Enemy speed increases at higher levels
Spawn points that can be sniped
questions.subject: game_design

questions.question: A well balanced game has which set of features?
choices: No dominant strategies, large numbers of options, and many ways to win
Equal numbers of player and AI forces, unit strength, and asymmetric starting positions
Transitive unit dominance, symbolic AI, and emergent game play
Progression base rules, esoteric interactions, and optimal integration systems
Complex interactions, player feedback, and problem solving
questions.subject: game_design

questions.question: Strong negative feedback will
choices: start players in the same place every game
provide better weapons as the game gets harder
ensures every player has the same chance of winning
create an unfair advantage for weak players
decrease the importance of the early part of a game
questions.subject: game_design

questions.question: If there was another option inserted into the confidence level for this multi-choice section (current options are +1.0, -0.0 ; +1.5, -0.5 and +2.0,-2.0), which values would result in all four options remaining the best choice for at least some levels of confidence? (i.e which option would not just replace one of the current options)
questions.subject: game_design
